data.title: Micron: Peak Cycle Margins And Unprecedented Capex Risk
data.description:
Introduction

Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: MU) has probably been one of the best performers on the market since I last gave it a Hold rating back in September. The stock has appreciated by over 131% based on various key decisions the company has made and the relentless rise in prices. With that in mind, I want to make the case for why I believe the valuation might have gone too far.

Right now, the company designs, manufactures, and sells memory and storage products worldwide and has been surfing the boom in demand for memories and HBMs based on the rise of AI.

Current Dynamics

The first recent major piece of news that we can substantially decipher would be the winding down of its consumer-centered SSD and RAM business, as to be able to shift production capacity toward high-margin AI data center products. The Crucial brand has been known as the gold standard for reliability, and the move directly tells us that Micron wants to instantly capitalize on the AI gold rush instead of its legacy business. Economically speaking, the move is understandable as HBM currently sells for a massive premium compared to a regular $50 stick of desktop RAM. Right now, Silicon wafer production is a zero-sum game as the insatiable demand from data centers forces producers to choose between production capacities. Micron won’t technically fully exit consumer RAM as it will still provide brands like Corsair and Kingston, but only after their high-priority AI and enterprise customers are satisfied. I believe that the move is inherently positive based on the current dynamics of the market, as Micron will focus on profitability over volume, pushing gross margins to nearly 60% in a commodity style of product segment.

I won’t do a full dive into the company’s last earnings as it is already old news, but it is important to highlight the fundamental shift in the company’s financial DNA. As it is prioritizing high-value AI components, the company has been able to achieve hyper-growth levels of profitability. The company has stated that its entire HBM supply for 2026 is already sold out, giving the company incredible pricing power, as highlighted by the incredible rise in prices. See production of HBM takes up around three times the wafer capacity of standard DDR5 RAM, which creates a voluntary shortage in the PC and smartphone markets, allowing prices to rise for consumer chips. This has pushed the company to make a shocking guidance with expected revenue set to reach $18.7B and gross margin to reach 68%, the highest level seen for the company and probably the industry.

And now the most recent rally could be related to the $100B megafab located in Clay, breaking ground that will be the company’s crown jewel of its domestic strategy, now being the largest private construction project in New York state history. The project will eventually house 4 fabs and will focus its production on leading-edge DRAM, the primary source of HBM that powers AI servers and next-generation cloud infrastructure. The project will be highly subsidized by the CHIPS and Science Act in a bid to push the country’s DRAM production to 40% of the global production within the next decade, taming away a crucial risk in the current AI supply chain.

All of these are positives in a way, but I believe that there is a potential substantial risk in betting the whole house on HBM for AI. First, if the industry cools down, then Micron won't easily flip a switch and revert to making consumer RAM. If, in the end, prices drop for Micron, then they will be left with a massive facility that produces 66% less stuff than it used to (hence the 3x wafer capacity required). More so, the company is spending $100B over 20 years, while semiconductor cycles been notoriously boom and bust. If the next phase of AI, deemed Agentic AI, does not produce immediate profits for companies like Microsoft and Google, the demand for $10,000 HBM modules will slow down. And then the company will be stuck with a half-finished 20-year project. Now, MU has shut down its shock absorber in Crucial, meaning that it has nowhere to dump extra inventory if its big enterprise partners suddenly cut orders. It has essentially cut their diverse customer base for a handful of hyperscalers, implying a huge concentration risk. The megafab is also making Micron now politically tied to the US government trade wars. If they are banned from selling certain tech to specific global markets, they won’t easily change their production elsewhere.

Valuation

In a world of expensive AI-related tech companies, I’ll say that Micron has a slightly too expensive valuation based on its P/E GAAP of 34.42, which is highly above the company’s historical average as the company pivoted towards AI. But as highlighted by the crash of 2023 that wiped out years of gains, there is the possibility of huge busts on the horizon for Micron, especially as its stock price becomes that much heightened. Just like in 2021, I believe that we’re in a panic-buying phase where companies like Nvidia and Amazon are terrified of running out of memory, but if they realize that they overbuilt their AI data center, then they will suddenly cancel orders where Micron would be left with billions in unsold, highly specialized HBM4 memory. Furthermore, Micron wants to spend $20B a year in CapEx, mainly for fabs. But if there were to be an AI winter, Micron would be stuck with $100B half-finished factories and no way to pay debt, while it is currently eating into its cash position.

In the near term, I expect growth to be tremendous for the company, as it has officially sold out its entire HBM capacity through the end of 2026, with customers like Nvidia and AMD only getting 50% to 66% of the memory they actually want from Micron. Moreover, Micron currently has HBM4 dominance, as they were the first to move the next-gen memory into mass production, capturing the highest-margin slice of the AI market before Samsung or SK Hynix can fully catch up. The Megafab that is poised to alleviate the manufacturing issues is a 20-year build-out that is already seeing signs of real-world friction with delays in production years and labor shortage, as it requires a large number of specialized workers. That’s why Micron fast-tracked its Boise, Idaho fab, which is expected to come online much sooner but at a greater cost at a time of already high CapEx.

Micron’s profitability right now is off the roof, to be fair, as the company is benefitting from extremely high-demand and limited supply capacity, but again, I do not expect these dynamics to continue for decades, while the company is planning it to. I expect rivals such as Samsung to ramp up their own AI memory production, hitting Micron’s current dominant position. The company is expecting to hit full volume in late 2026, while they will likely start a price war to gain market share, in my opinion, forcing Micron's margins back down, and I do not believe that the market has been pricing this for the company. That’s why I do not believe that MU has a real Moat protecting its margins, as it is only protected by a supply shortage and not better technology that will be essentially caught up by competitors.

Conclusion

Micron is a great company, yes, and in a sense, the valuation metrics right now could make the case for an investment. But diving into the details and the fundamentals, I do not see how investors should invest in the company at over $360 a share. Competition is prone to catch up at a time when it is spending left and right to capture the most of a transitory period. In that sense, I would recommend shareholders to sell their shares right now, hence I give a Sell rating for Micron Technology.

data.title: Alger Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Capital Appreciation Fund underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Western Digital Corporation (WDC), and Natera, Inc. (NTRA) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Western Digital is a hard disk drive (HDD) storage company benefiting from rising hyperscaler data-center investment and the continued proliferation of data. The HDD industry is highly consolidated, with only two scaled manufacturers, and Western Digital holds a leading market position. The business has structurally shifted toward cloud customers as consumer exposure has declined, with cloud representing the majority of Western Digital’s revenue. Importantly, industry participants have emphasized capital discipline—prioritizing higher areal density (i.e., more terabytes per drive) rather than adding significant unit capacity—which supports a healthier supply/demand balance and improved profitability. During the quarter, shares contributed positively as the company reported operating results that validated improving fundamentals, including strong revenue and earnings growth driven by higher unit volumes, better-than-guided drive density, favorable pricing, and lower cost per terabyte that helped expand margins.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group (NBIS), Meta Platforms Inc (META), and Sea Limited (SE) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Meta is the world’s largest social-media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (capex) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

Sea Limited is a global consumer internet company operating three core businesses: Garena, a digital entertainment platform known for developing and publishing online games; Shopee, a leading e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia and Taiwan; and SeaMoney, which offers digital financial services including mobile wallets and payment processing. The company has benefited from strong structural growth in its e-commerce and payments business, along with its gaming business returning to stabilization after years of decline. Shares detracted from performance as investors questioned the pace and durability of Sea’s profitability improvement. Specifically, its largest e-commerce competitor in Latin America lowered free-shipping thresholds, which may increase competitive intensity and pressure margins in the near- term.

data.title: IEUR: Financials Levered To European Downgrade
data.description:
The iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (IEUR) is a USD-denominated ETF that plays the market in Europe, dominated by European financials but also with quite a lot of exposure to the net exporting European industrial and healthcare sector. The IEUR headline performance isn't so bad but only because the USD has gotten weaker while the European market has also gotten weaker as the market confronts another period of tariff threats but now over Greenland.

The nature of this phase of tariffs is more threatening for the USD because it further puts into question the global order, with the U.S. increasingly at odds with NATO and demonstrating little effective trust in the content of the NATO alliance. Moreover, a renewed tariff issue also effectively tears up previously achieved tariffs deals between Europe and the U.S. Financials are exposed due to their indexation to macro, and the rest of European equities often depend on U.S. markets as the destination of their products.

On the other hand, there will probably be a resolution to this as far as markets are concerned. Whether it will be because Trump acknowledges that in effect it already has clear access to Greenland without needing to own it or some other new agreement, we do think this gets resolved. We'd probably not buy the dip though as we believe the whole world is exposed to a possible turn down in the AI boost to the U.S. and consequently Western economy. We continue to focus on idiosyncratic markets.

IEUR Breakdown

In our previous coverage of IEUR we mentioned that the EUR was a better bet than the USD. This remains the case with alternative currencies to the USD and of course surging bullion prices a testament to how upturns of the geopolitical order are not good for the USD. Some of these dynamics are relevant now as well as of course the Greenland issue is clearly interacting with assumptions around the geopolitical order.

The threat currently is a 10% tariff starting on February 1st, on all exports of a few EU but not all EU countries, but effectively all EU countries due to the features of the EU as a trade bloc. These could rise further in June to 25% if the U.S. is not allowed to buy Greenland ostensibly from the Danish Kingdom, though of course Greenland already is fairly autonomous, and they have their own ideas of what their status should be as a territory, though it appears quite clear that they aren't keen on being a 51st state. Denmark does not want to sell Greenland.

The reason why Greenland is being discussed at all is because it's valuable real estate in the Atlantic, and would be an important extension of U.S. naval presence in the Atlantic and a way to rival Russia in the Arctic. The issue is that Denmark is already in NATO and the U.S. already can project itself using Greenland through the alliance, with the Danish in tow who have been enthusiastic and voluntary U.S. allies in quite a few conflicts in the past. With the threat of even military force at some point should a deal not be reached, other than questions about whether Denmark will be as enthusiastic as it used to be as a U.S. ally, at least while Trump is in office, there is an implicit distrust in NATO demonstrated by U.S. actions who don't seem to feel that NATO is enough to guarantee the U.S.' ability to project itself into the Arctic and the Atlantic.

This is probably one trading factor that will punish the USD, particularly as the EU starts to float their own anti-coercion mechanisms, which can range from some sort of duty on U.S. services but also to more bespoke measures that may threaten capital flows that sustain the U.S. deficit which is what is giving the U.S. leverage over Europe due to the healthier European current account dependent on U.S. consumption.

Economic integration is what makes the USD an effective utility and underwrites its value and the exorbitant privilege of it being the world's reserve currency. Threats to that are always bad for the dollar, and the additional risk of military disintegration could further exacerbate speculation that Europe may have to court others to diversify their economic and even military ties.

The risks to the USD should support interest in an ETF like IEUR, but IEUR is still challenged in these scenarios.

Sectoral exposure to financials indexes the ETF significantly to macro considerations and the European downgrade, and tariffs are a threat to European macro hence their use in coercion around the Greenland issue by the U.S. Then of course industrials are directly threatened, as well as healthcare although to a lesser extent, both also large exposures. Important to note is that these tariffs may reset the trade deal with Europe. It's unclear how they would react with the previously exempted goods and the previous deal in general. Moreover, the EU countries have secured a deal better than the 25% threatened eventually in June and would have a lot to lose at that point.

Bottom Line

The market is risking a reset to the narrative and dynamics of Liberation Day. IEUR is not really safe from that, with macro exposure and direct tariff exposure offsetting the benefit of the underlying businesses trading in EUR and not USD. In the short term, we do believe that these issues will be resolved and that the effects of the tariff won't go through. That could be a dip buying opportunity, but longer term we are not particularly keen on Europe because we see them as broadly exposed to the health of the U.S. economy which remains a little uncertain, particularly as it is being buoyed by AI which we believe is an unsustainable trend. Other markets with less overall exposure to US-EU macro that could trade down may be worth buying on the dip assuming that much like previous trade and geopolitical disputes over the Atlantic, they usually resolve in a benign way.

But the point is to get away from the USD while President Trump is in office, and we are interested in recession resistant exposures in developed by more disconnected geographies like Australia to avoid further risks.

data.title: Alger Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Communication Services. The largest sector overweight was Utilities and the largest sector underweight was Health Care.

The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Communication Services and Health Care were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index- level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure.

Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

data.title: CURE: Benefits And Caveats Of The Leveraged Healthcare ETF
data.description:
Fast facts and strategy of the Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3x Shares ETF

The Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3X Shares ETF (CURE) is a leveraged ETF with a factor of 3x in the healthcare sector. CURE was launched on June 15, 2011 and has a net expense ratio of 0.93%. It is a small, but quite liquid ETF, with $180 million of AUM (assets under management) and a 3-month average daily dollar volume of $9 million. The issuer Direxion is specialized in leveraged ETFs, with dozens of these products based on equity indexes and single stocks.

CURE is designed to deliver 300% of the daily performance of the Health Care Select Sector Index, which is the underlying index of the State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV), often used as a sector benchmark. Its -3X bear counterpart (ticker SICK) was delisted in September 2017. Direxion gives a clear warning on CURE:

The fund should not be expected to provide three times or negative three times the return of the benchmark’s cumulative return for periods greater than a day.

Indeed, the daily leverage factor is a source of drift, a topic that I will develop more in this article. The prospectus is more specific:

The Fund will lose money if the Index performance is flat over time, and as a result of daily rebalancing, the Index’s volatility and the effects of compounding, it is even possible that the Fund will lose money over time while the Index’s performance increases over a period longer than a single day.

The fund invests in financial instruments that, in combination, provide 3X daily leveraged exposure to the underlying index. The most commonly used instruments are stocks, swaps and futures contracts related to the index. Keeping a daily objective requires rebalancing the portfolio on a daily basis. Direxion reports a portfolio turnover rate of 23% in the most recent fiscal year, excluding transactions on cash instruments or derivatives. The total turnover rate is not disclosed.

Underlying index and portfolio

The Health Care Select Sector Index includes constituents of the S&P 500 Index classified by GICS in the healthcare sector. The sector is composed of the following industries: Pharmaceuticals, Biotechnology, Life Sciences Tools & Services, Health Care Equipment & Supplies, Health Care Providers & Services, Health Care Technology. The index is weighted based on float-adjusted market capitalization and rebalanced on a quarterly basis. As of writing, it is composed of 60 stocks and is concentrated, with significant risk related to the largest companies. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed below based on XLV data, represent 57.6% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 14.4%.

Competitors

There is no other leveraged ETF based on the same index, but some leveraged ETFs are related to the sector:

  • Direxion Daily S&P Biotech Bull 3X Shares ETF (LABU) has a factor of 3x on an equal-weighted index focused on the biotechnology industry.
  • ProShares Ultra Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (BIB) has a factor of 2x on a market-cap weighted index in the same industry.
  • Direxion Daily Biotech Top 5 Bull 2X ETF (TBXU) has a factor of 2x on an equal-weighted index concentrated in the five largest biotech companies (this fund was launched in 2025).

Such performance inconsistency points to path-dependent drift, raising a question:

Why do leveraged ETFs drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta-slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, management costs. Beta-slippage is the main reason in equity leveraged ETFs such as CURE. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. On the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1

In the same time, the perfectly leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta-slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%:

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21

The perfect 2X leveraged ETFs is up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44

Beta-slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one third of its value, instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667

With this demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the higher the decay.

Also, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the drift. If we come back to the first example, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged. The good news is that a higher factor also magnifies positive drift in uptrends. In the second example (underlying index gaining 10% two days in a row), the 3X ETF gains 69%, while the 2X ETF is up 44% and the underlying index is up 21%.

(1 + 0.3) x (1 + 0.3) = 1.69

Drift calculation and history

There is no standard definition for the drift of a leveraged ETF. Mine is simple and based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index on a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. First, let’s start by defining “Return”: it is the total return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval. “Index Return” is the total return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval, calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (Index Return x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

The next chart plots the 12-month drift of CURE from June 15, 2012 (one year after inception) to January 16, 2026.

The 12-month drift has been negative most of the time since August 2015 and its historical average is -2.26%. This is the average annual decay normalized to the underlying index exposure. As the leverage factor is 3x, the average annual decay of CURE itself is -6.78%.

Takeaway: who should consider CURE?

CURE is well-suited for trading and tactical strategies with clear entry and exit signals. Holding CURE for the long term may work, but historical data show that it is likely to result in deep drawdowns and long periods of underperformance. Additionally, as drift adds some noise in the price action of CURE, traders should base their technical analysis and indicators on the underlying index, or most conveniently on XLV.

This article answers these three main questions about CURE:

  1. What are the risks to investing in CURE?
  2. Is CURE meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  3. What kind of indicators are usable to trade CURE?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Glencore and Venezuelan Oil: Who Wins? Who Loses?
data.description:
The first recipient of largesse from the seizure of Venezuelan oil assets has been a private company, Vitol. The contract is worth about $250 million. Vitol is the world’s largest independent oil trader, though Glencore (GLNCY) is the largest publicly-traded oil trader (and also deals in many more commodities, as well.)

Some have noted that one of Vitol’s senior traders was a major campaign contributor to President Trump, but the White House says it was not an issue. And since Vitol is the largest independent oil trader, it is a logical choice to begin distributing the oil in the most efficient and profitable way.

I take no stance on the above; I merely note that the first winner is already out of the gate and point out that this process will continue to move quickly.

If you make investing decisions based on politics, you are making a poor decision. If you make investing decisions based on potential for investment success, like which public companies stand to benefit, the time to consider them is now.

What Makes Venezuelan Oil Valuable to the US?

There are various types of oil, some of which flow easily and are shipped without significant additional steps. Others require more complex work and pose greater engineering challenges in moving the product from the drilling site to the refinery.

Do not be overly impressed when some reporter gushes that Venezuela has the highest proven oil reserves of any nation in the world.

Having reserves and getting to them at a cost that allows you to make a profit are two very different things!

Witness the USA, which has about one-fourth the “reserves” of Canada and about one-seventh the proven reserves of Venezuela -- and is the world’s largest exporter of oil.

State-of-the-art drilling equipment, hydraulic fracturing, horizontal drilling, superb ability to get the product to market, and more, are clearly more important than mere “reserves.”

Also, there is a sliding scale of the type of oil that is readily drill-able. The two primary designators of these different types of oil are “light sweet crude” and “heavy sour crude.” All oil destined for refining falls somewhere within this range.

Don’t get wrapped around the axle trying to delve deeply into the chemistry of all this… the designation of light versus heavy is basically the American Petroleum Institute’s (API's) way of determining the density of crude oil in relation to the density of water and the amount of sulfurous byproduct that must be removed.

The measure used to determine density is called “API gravity.” If the API gravity is greater than 10, the oil floats on water. If it’s less than 10, the oil sinks. When you think of oil spills in the ocean or on a lake, this factor helps explain why, sometimes, the oil can be skimmed away, and other times it forms into a larger mass and remains an issue for a longer time.

There is one more determinant of light versus heavy: the oil’s sulfur content. All oil, before refining, contains some sulfur. Light sweet crude typically contains less than 0.5% sulfur; heavy sour crude contains more than 1%.

A good example of light sweet crude is the oil in almost the entire Permian Basin in West Texas. Add fracking and horizontal drilling, and you have a very profitable source of oil that can be easily sent via pipelines to refineries relatively close by along the Gulf Coast.

A good example of heavy sour crude is… Venezuelan oil. It is a viscous sludge before treatment. Treatment consists of several steps. It is most often heated to coax it into moving. This requires external heating, which adds to the cost and requires an additional energy source. Ideally, it can then be diluted by mixing it with less viscous oils or chemicals (perhaps imported? If so, another expense).

Sturdier, more specialized (more expensive) pumps are typically used at the drill site. Then there is the cost of high-capacity equipment and heated systems to prevent oil from coagulating during loading, transportation, and unloading at the port, which then flows through pipelines to the nearest refineries. Along the way, there are additional expenses associated with this corrosive substance, as well as the cost of renting the tankers.

So, Why Doesn’t the US Just Use Local Sweet Crude?

A fair question. The answer: The United States has not always enjoyed the benefits of light sweet crude.

Remember Peak Oil? The US was kowtowing to Saudi Arabia and anyone else that would save us from this no-more-energy doomsday scenario. I wrote an investment publication sent via US mail in those pre-computer days. Even then, I was reading oil industry publications about new discoveries in the USA, as I regularly jabbed holes in the Peak Oil brouhaha.

The wildcatters who were finding the light sweet crude that put the nail in the coffin of Peak Oil reaped its rewards. But here was their problem:

It takes years and beaucoup bucks to build a refinery complex. Those of you who live near or frequently pass by one of these understand – these are huge, expensive, not always easy to maintain, behemoths. And they were built to refine the oil the US had typically had within its borders, which, when built, meant heavy sour crude.

This is still mostly the case for US refineries.

In fact, much of the light crude is exported from the US to other nations with refineries designed to handle this sweeter-smelling oil, such as Saudi Arabia and Nigeria. (Yes, they already have oil, but they can make even more money by selling the refined product to others not so fortunate.)

What About the US's Neighbors, Canada and Mexico?

It remains to be seen whether they, too, will find sweet crude. Canada is a huge nation, the second largest in the world, and I would venture to say they have barely scratched the surface of what might await them, be it oil, gas, gold, silver, copper, rare metals, and so on.

But for now, Canada has only discovered, in any size, heavy crude like Alberta’s Western Canadian Select. It, too, needs all the extra steps to reach refineries. It is not sent by sea, but by pipeline to many US refineries. Instead of heating the product while at sea, they have to keep the pipelines warm enough to pump the oil.

It is the same story with Mexico. Both on land and in the Gulf of Mexico, thus far, Mexico’s primary contribution has been heavy oil, brought to the USA primarily by undersea and land pipelines, with some tanker (and even rail) delivery.

So, Who Can Use That Venezuelan Oil Right Now?

The aforementioned oil traders and US oil refineries. And not just any US oil refineries – specifically, those situated along the US Gulf Coast, on a direct sea route from Venezuela.

Most major oil traders are private firms. The only one of serious size that is public is Glencore plc (GLNCY).

Glencore, headquartered in Switzerland and traded actively in London and the US, is most often thought of as a major global mining and metals company, but it is also an oil (and other energy sources) trader. There is talk of a merger with Rio Tinto (RIO), a company I have owned for our Investor’s Edge ® subscribers. I recently bought Glencore just last month, not because of any merger, but because I believe it is a brilliant company in a powerful position at the right time.

When I compare the Big Three refiners along the US Gulf Coast, Valero Energy (VLO), Marathon (MPC), and Phillips 66 (PSX) to Glencore, all four look as if they could benefit from Venezuelan oil. I call them the Big Three because Valero has a market cap of $55 billion, as does Marathon. Phillips 66 capitalization is about $35 billion.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant ranks these three refiners as Buys. They are numbers 2,3, and 8 in the Top Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks rankings:

All three are ranked as “Buy” or “Strong Buy” from SA Quant. All three could benefit from errant shadow-fleet tankers or from diplomatically agreed-upon legal flows going forward. My best guess is that they will each see “their share” of Venezuelan oil production.

Glencore, on the other hand, is ranked by SA Quant as just 2 steps above a “hold” rating.

I disagree with this analysis!

Part of SA Quant’s take is because Glencore is only listed as a Diversified Metals and Mining company. Even if GLNCY were not a major global oil trader and much more, if the company were “just” a $70 billion Materials Sector giant, I would own it. Being part of the key traders trading Venezuelan oil will, I believe, be the icing on the cake.

The cake itself gives us a company that will do just fine even if the flow of Venezuelan oil is only a trickle for now.

Who Wins If There Is a Windfall of Oil Flowing from Venezuela?

The Big Three refiners, and perhaps other smaller ones, will likely share some of the increased oil flow, meaning additional profit (albeit perhaps rather small at the beginning).

If my analysis is wrong and virtually no oil will flow until 2027, the refiners will still get along just fine with their current oil supply and income.

Especially Glencore.

I believe, however, that Glencore, with its solid reputation and ability to move large volumes of product, will be awarded oil trading contracts. But Glencore, with or without substantial oil trading contracts, will still be a winner.

Glencore is deeply entrenched in both upstream and downstream markets. The upstream markets comprise their mining and drilling divisions and partnerships; the downstream markets comprise their trading and the infrastructure that makes that trading most powerful.

That infrastructure includes ownership of storage facilities, railways, and even seagoing vessels, including bulk carriers, container ships, and, yes, crude oil tankers. These include specialized double-hull tankers equipped to handle heavy crude.

Symbiosis? A Glencore subsidiary could even be among the tankers used to bring the oil to the US refineries.

About the upstream side of Glencore: base metals (the much larger “other side” of precious metals) are booming. Glencore is actively mining its substantial reserves of aluminum, coal, copper, lead, nickel, tin, and zinc, as well as small amounts of precious metals that accompany the mining and can be profitably separated as an adjunct to the larger mining processes.

Every one of these metals rose in value in 2025. In fact, the metals that GLNCY mines and the large universe of agricultural and energy products that the company trades, are in a bullish trend.

This key because, as a broad sweep, metals and mining account for roughly 55% of Glencore's revenue.

Oil accounts for another 30%.

And agricultural trading in all kinds of grains and oilseeds like soybeans, canola, and peanuts accounts for most of the remaining 15%.

Geographically, from where does Glencore derive its revenue?

Everywhere.

As you would expect, Africa, as the latest entrant into deep mining exploration, provides the largest share of both mining and trading, at about 35%.

What you might not expect is that 25% comes from Europe, where you would think they are already all played out. Not so. Eastern Europe and, particularly, the far north of Norway, Sweden, and Finland, are finding surprisingly large deposits of base metals, rare earths, and more.

North America comes next, at 20%. Given Canada's vast geography, I expect to see considerable growth in North America, both in mining and in oil and gas exploration and discovery.

Ditto for South America, currently at 15%. The world has barely scratched the surface of some of South America's "outback" areas.

And speaking of the Outback, the Asia/Pacific region provides about 5% of GLNCY's revenue, with Australia alone accounting for the lion's share.

The world needs what Glencore does and what it has.

The current US administration’s belief that lower rates mean a cornucopia without any downside is misplaced. Lower rates are helpful in some ways, of course. But the inevitable result of forcing rates down from a political rather than an economic perspective results in continued high inflation. This means higher prices for Glencore’s metals.

Tariffs are another factor in Glencore’s favor. While US tariffs on foreign nations’ exports to the US serve as a tax on US importers and ultimately lead to higher prices for US citizens, they also cause significant distortions from reasonable pricing, creating greater opportunities for companies accustomed to operating in such environments. Like Glencore.

In short, Glencore’s experience and familiarity with mining, processing, transporting, and trading give it a perspective few other companies can match.

Is there a risk to owning Glencore? There is no reward without some risk. The risk to Glencore is the same risk we all face. If poor decisions drive nations into recession, there will be less mining, less building, less transport, and less trading. Until I see the likelihood of an imminent recession, I will continue to own Glencore.

Venezuelan Oil: Who Loses?

As a geopolitical analyst, I view countries and regions to see who loses. First, if the largesse is shared not with the Venezuelan people but given to the current Friends of Nicolas Maduro, who still run the country with an iron fist, the Venezuelans lose, and the US loses the respect and support of every nation in this hemisphere. (If you are interested in the geopolitics at play here, I have written more about this under my name on Substack.)

If Venezuela becomes a democratic nation freed from the dictatorship, summary executions, and secret police of the current regime, there is one loser whose loss is even more important to the Free World: China.

On every trip I make to Central or South America, I see more evidence of Chinese BRI (Belt & Road Initiative) infrastructure and influence. These ports, dams, highways, railways, and other infrastructure are not provided gratis. The countries that accept the assistance are loaned the money that China decides the project is worth. When the loans come due, they must pay up or give something important away. The graphic below shows the 37 ports that China now operates, controls, or owns outright, with the closest to the US highlighted.

I will be doing some research in the Southern Cone of South America in four weeks. I will report any significant changes I see upon my return.

China has had a free hand in Latin America for too long. China not only has control of some infrastructure in Venezuela, but it is the prime recipient of discounted Venezuelan oil shipped across the ocean.

China must now face the fact that it can no longer place dual-use (civilian and military) facilities in the United States’ backyard.

Some analysts have said Canada, which currently ships sour crude to the US via pipelines, will suffer from the competition from Venezuela.

I disagree. If Canada loses some contracts in the coming years, it can supply China. China will pay more, of course, and the Canadians, America’s best ally through many wars, will own all the ports and infrastructure.

Losers?

China.

Winners?

Probably the refiners along the US Gulf Coast.

Certainly Glencore.

Respectfully submitted,

Joseph L. Shaefer

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BOK Financial: Continued Upside Momentum
data.description:
We pick back up with our Q4 regional bank earnings season coverage today with BOK Financial Corporation (BOKF). Again, we like to look at regional bank performance to see if there are any signs of economic weakness. Typically, we find economic weakness (or a renewed wave of economic strength) can be found from examining the composite results of regional banks. While regional banks come with their own pockets of strength and weaknesses, given the geographic variance between so many of them, when combined we can start to see if there are momentum shifts happening.

So far, we are very early in the reporting season, but strength continues, at least in reports we have seen so far. The only slight negative we have noted in the early goings is a bit of softening in some of the asset quality metrics but so far, nothing of major concern. A look at BOK Financial gives us some insight into the Southwest and Midwest of the United States.

In short, the bank had a great 2025, and the Q4 report showed ongoing momentum in this quality hold. We continue to see strength here. Let us discuss the just-reported Q4 earnings and the key metrics we follow for the regional banking space.

BOK Financial Q4 Earnings Headline Numbers Strong

Thus far, regional bank revenues have risen from last year. We saw the same for BOK Financial. Revenues were up 12.6% from last year, hitting $592 million. This was also a sequential increase. Factoring in the increases in both interest and fee-based sources of income as well as and operating expenses which actually were down from last year, net income came in at $177.3 million, or $2.89 per share. This was much better than consensus expectations, and the EPS figure was $0.67 higher than last year. A record quarter, and a record year, with $9.17 in EPS. We also want to add that there were no loan loss provisions here in Q4.

BOK Financial's Loan And Deposit Growth In Q4

We also assess trends in loans and deposit for regional banks. Changes in trends in both of these key metrics can give us insight into economic strength and weakness. A trend of lower deposits could suggest economic need for cash, as well as possible competition, while lower loan demand usually stems from a slowing economy. Of course, one quarter does not make a trend.

Well, sequentially, we saw growth in both loans and deposits. Loans grew $786 million from the sequential quarter, hitting $25.7 billion. The bank is also growing its deposits once again, as deposits rose $935 million from the sequential quarter, at $39.4 billion. This is strong in our opinion and shows continued momentum for BOK Financial.

We also saw better margins once again this quarter. Net interest margin expanded 7 basis points thanks to a lower cost of funds, which was down 27 basis points, however the yields on loans also dipped, stemming from lower interest rates for both. The net interest margin came in at 2.98%. A widening margin helps revenue and earnings power, of course.

BOK Financial's Asset Quality

As we noted above, so far, early in this Q4 reporting season we have seen some slight softening in asset quality. While the sample size is small thus far, it is notable. We saw stability for the most part in Q4 on several asset quality metrics that we follow. At the end of Q4, nonperforming assets were just $75 million, $1 million higher than in Q3. That said, the ratio of nonperforming assets to total loans improved to 0.29% from 0.30% in Q3.

Net charge-offs were just $1.4 million, down from $3.6 million in Q3. This is just 0.02%, a 4 basis point improvement from 0.06% of all loans in the sequential quarter. The allowance for credit losses remains modest at $327 million, $1 million lower than Q3, but was a 4 basis point improvement from 1.32% of all loans to just 1.28%. With the huge EPS increase and better margins, it is not surprising that return metrics were mixed improved. The return on average assets rose 28 basis point to 1.36% from 1.08%. The return on average equity surged 242 basis points, to 11.80%. Finally, the efficiency ratio improved from 66.6% efficiency ratio to 60.71%, the best it was all year.

Take Home

Despite some softening in asset quality early on in the reporting season, BOK Financial showed no signs of weakness. Nearly every metric improved in the quarter, including those not discussed in the article like expanding book value, and the dividend payout ratio. This quarter saw very well-controlled expenses and higher loans and deposits. Asset quality, of course, was strong. While ongoing improvement is difficult given Q4 will set up a tough comp, BOK stock remains a quality Hold.

data.title: Drawdowns Never Take A Vacation: Investors Should Expect Steep Corrections, Even In Up Years
data.description:
By James Picerno

Managing expectations is one of the biggest investment challenges, especially as it relates to risk. Many stock market investors tend to underestimate the potential for short-term losses. That’s an oversight that threatens to derail an otherwise solid portfolio strategy if fear suddenly triggers a bout of selling at or near a market bottom.

The solution starts with developing reasonable assumptions about what’s possible, based on the market’s track record. A useful starting point is reviewing the history of drawdowns, which measure market corrections from the previous peaks. Even during bull markets, peak-to-trough declines can be relatively steep, a fact that may surprise investors.

Some of the deepest drawdowns occurred in years when the S&P 500 Index rose during the calendar year, indicated by black bars in the chart below. Last year is an example: the market rallied more than 16% in 2025. A solid gain, but that didn’t prevent the market from enduring a steep intra-year drawdown of nearly -19%.

For a clearer summary of how the deepest drawdowns by calendar years compare through time, the boxplot below consolidates the historical results since 1931. The median is nearly -20% (black line). The interquartile range since is roughly -10% to -31% (shown in the box), which represents the most typical declines, statistically speaking.

For the past 20 years, the interquartile range is a bit milder at -8% to -18%. But these drawdowns are still a reminder that a bull trend offers little, if any, immunity to sharp setbacks in the short term.

On that basis, what should we expect for 2026? More of the same is the baseline outlook, history advises. Since 2000, for example, most calendar years experienced at least one double-digit drawdown - only five calendar years beat the odds and posted a maximum drawdown no deeper than 8.5%.

The lesson is that a white-knuckle ride, even during a bull market, is a hardy perennial in the stock market. Preparing for these events, emotionally and otherwise, is essential to profit from equities in a meaningful degree over the long haul.

Meanwhile, the year is still young and the deepest drawdown so far in 2026 is a mere -1.1%. If history is a guide, we’re likely to see a much deeper shade of red at some point before the year is out, even if the bulls keep running.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Jack In The Box: When The 'Ugly Duckling' Stays Ugly (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Jack in the Box Inc. (JACK) turned out to be one of my weaker calls in 2024 and early 2025. The "Ugly Ducklings" in FSRs simply outshone their QSR counterparts.

Dine Brands (DIN) is a textbook case of this strategy paying off in full-service restaurants.

This went beyond the asset-light model and leaned on value platforms that somehow delivered way more bang for the buck than typical fast-food deals (think "Really Big Meal Deal" and "2 for $25").

That’s why, when I put together the 2026 "Ultimate Guide" for limited-service, I honed in on obsessive operators like Yum Brands (YUM) and growing names like Dutch Bros (BROS), following Potbelly’s playbook from last year—and it paid off handsomely.

With about a 40% drop since I called it Strong Buy last time, would doubling down on that strategy be downright silly?

Well, I've been mulling this over lately.

After digging through earnings calls and reading what other analysts here at Seeking Alpha are saying, the core thesis hasn't shifted much, aside from the Del Taco sale.

Until Q4 FY 2025 (Jack In The Box's latest earnings, which still had Del Taco in its portfolio), the same problems were there:

  1. Same-store sales didn’t just keep falling in FY 2025, they actually accelerated their decline, hitting -7.1% in Q3 and -7.4% in Q4. That came even against easier comps and some small gains in QSRs as guests, especially those with tighter budgets, started acting more defensive.
  2. For more than two years they've been pushing barbell pricing and a value play, something McDonald's (MCD) got right with McValue and Wendy's (WEN) has had locked in for a while with the Biggie Bag. Even running combos like Bonus Jack and Smashed Jack for $5, most of their restaurants still charge 10 to 20% more thanks to their California-heavy footprint (don't forget AB 1228, which hiked fast-food minimum wages there by $20 an hour).
  3. Restaurants are still closing. Jack In The Box has 2,136 locations, nearly half in California, and under the "Jack on Track" program they’re shutting underperformers to keep the system healthy, much like Wendy’s did. That’s a 2.5% drop in units versus last year, not counting Del Taco, which lost about 3% and would’ve lost more without buying some franchisee spots.

With dividends and buybacks on pause, management is clearly zeroing in on one problem they can actually tackle: debt.

That debt has already topped about 6x Net Debt/EBITDA, a hefty load even for an asset-light company.

So the billion-dollar question is whether "Jack on Track" can actually get things moving again and if the stock is cheap enough to make a speculative buy worth it, as a lot of folks are saying.

I could be wrong, of course, like last time, but I think it's worth giving this thesis a fresh look.

Why Trouble Still Looms for Jack In the Box

On top of the usual QSR gap, Jack In The Box has had to deal with higher prices, which keeps traffic down in California. Some sources even point to deportations as a factor weighing on traffic in certain zip codes.

Everyone knows this, and it definitely had an impact in the first half of last year.

But you know what's even more curious? The same-store sales gap between Jack In The Box and other QSR Burger locations has only increased since the second half of the year.

Here's a graph to help you understand better:

The picture is pretty clear. The gap at Jack In The Box stretched from around 700 bp in Q1 to about 820 bp in Q2, and it stuck at that level in Q3 compared with the benchmark.

Digging into geolocation traffic data for California, Jack has been trailing other chains for quite some time, and that softness didn't let up in December either.

Now, if California headwinds are hitting everyone, shouldn’t traffic look more evenly distributed?

Yet month after month since July, Jack In The Box has been posting weaker traffic than its peers and even below the broader California QSR average.

We know how this played out. The real question is why.

To start with, management seems convinced the gap comes down to a weaker value proposition versus peers. The logic is pretty straightforward. As the price war heated up, the weakest link started giving up share, and in this case, that link was Jack In The Box.

On the value side, Jack ends up caught in the middle. It lacks McDonald’s mass appeal, Wendy’s reliability, Burger King’s (QSR) flexibility, and Taco Bell’s innovation.

They’re famous for owning late night. What I underestimated last year is that even those discretionary late-night trips would soften as consumers pulled back. I even wrote a few pieces about breakfast, another discretionary occasion, and late night ended up walking down the very same road.

Another angle is that food quality has faded, particularly in sauces, while the marketing strayed from the old Jack mascot and its more guerrilla-like tone. The last time they really pulled off a strong campaign, as far as I can tell, was around the Deadpool & Wolverine release.

Of course, higher prices are part of the story too. But in Jack's case, that's actually less of a smoking gun, since they were among the QSRs that pushed pricing the least coming out of the pandemic.

That sounds comforting at first, but on second thought, it's also a bit of a red flag, because the traffic gap versus other QSRs is still sitting there.

To put some numbers around it, a few sources show that Jack In The Box raised prices about 45% from late 2019 to mid-2024 across its five core menu items.

That’s well below McDonald’s, which saw a staggering 141.4%, as well as Taco Bell at 57.4%, Chick-fil-A at 80.1%, and several others.

To me, leaning into combos and full-price items on the core menu is what's actually working in this space, and that's exactly where Jack needs to be aiming.

Unit-Level Economy: Red Flag?

I can't even recall if I've crunched the unit-level numbers for the average Jack In the Box, but when stores start closing, that's always something worth digging into.

To start with, it's worth noting that the average Jack In the Box unit is doing about $1.87 million in AUV, roughly 3.6% lower than last year, with restaurant-level margins sitting at 16.1%. That’s down from 18.5% a year ago and still below the 17% to 18% range management is aiming for in FY 2026.

Newer markets like Chicago can push north of $2 million in annualized sales, but those stores also tend to run with tighter margins.

Most of the estimates I've come across put a new box somewhere between $1.9 million and $4 million. I’ll split the difference and use about $2.95 million as a rough midpoint for the initial investment.

I'm going to take two different types of prototypes here:

  • Current prototype: AUV of $1.87 million and a margin of 16.1%, which gives us a restaurant-level EBITDA of ~$301,000;
  • Prototype projected for FY 2026: AUV of $2 million and a margin of 17.5%, with a restaurant-level EBITDA of ~$350,000.

Put it all together, and under today's conditions a typical Jack In the Box is delivering below-average QSR returns, especially when you stack it up against build-to-suit concepts like Potbelly, where store-level IRRs can easily run in the 20% to 30% range depending on the market.

And the real issue here isn’t the AUVs.

The top quartile of Jack units can push up toward $2.5 million, but even Taco Bell’s average AUVs don’t really run much higher than that. Jack’s main issue is weak restaurant-level margins for a QSR, with AB 1228 eating up about 33.7% of sales.

Jack on Track

These days, the entire investment story basically hangs on the ‘Jack on Track’ plan to clean up the business and the balance sheet.

We already know what's broken, so it makes more sense to start with what management says they're actually trying to fix.

On the operations side, the plan really boils down to fixing four loose ends:

  1. To get leaders to spend twice as much time inside restaurants.
  2. Try to consistently stabilize food quality across franchisees.
  3. Make small remodels in a handful of older stores, mostly in California, just to see what sticks before rolling out a bigger refresh once franchisees are ready.
  4. Shutting down underperforming locations.

Before any of that could happen, though, one thing had to come first: selling Del Taco.

Squeezed on one side by Taco Bell’s momentum and on the other by family-owned and small regional chains, Del Taco also got hit by AB 1228, with 94 of its 132 remaining stores sitting in California.

The end result was more than two straight years of negative same-store sales and some truly ugly profitability, about 6.8% in Q4 FY 2025.

And of course, pulling off a turnaround is hard enough on its own, let alone trying to fix two at the same time! But beyond the operational cleanup, I also see the sale of Del Taco as a good way to bring leverage down and clean up the balance sheet.

I know this isn't exactly fresh news anymore, but taking a closer look at the deal still adds a lot of value to the story.

To start with, Del Taco was picked up by Yadav Enterprises.

If that name rings a bell, it’s because they were also involved in the Denny’s (DENN) transaction back in November. Yadav is a multi-brand operator with exposure to concepts like Jack In the Box, TGI Friday’s, and El Pollo Loco (LOCO), and now runs close to 400 locations across the U.S.

The deal came in at $115 million, a massive haircut versus the roughly $580 million Jack paid for the brand just four years ago.

When you stack that against Del Taco’s $311.3 million in revenue, you’re talking about a sales multiple of roughly 0.36x. That’s bargain-basement territory for a restaurant brand and a pretty clear signal that Del Taco had slipped into a distressed-asset.

Just to put that in context, the average EV/S multiple for restaurant deals between 2010 and 2025 sits around 1.7x (Del Taco implies a discount of over 78% of that).

On the deleveraging front, Jack In The Box would end up with roughly $166.5 million in pro-forma cash against about $1.7 billion of debt, putting net debt around $1.53 billion.

Management is guiding to adjusted EBITDA in the $225 to $240 million range, and even at the midpoint, Net Debt/EBITDA still sits north of 6x.

That’s a heavy load, even for an asset-light model.

If you ask me, the path forward isn't complicated, but it isn't easy either.

Jack In The Box needs to claw its way back to the FCF it used to generate before Del Taco, roughly $150 million a year or a touch below that. EBITDA also needs to heal back toward its old run-rate, closer to $300 million annually.

Do that consistently for a couple of years, and Net Debt/EBITDA can realistically slide toward the 4x range, which starts to feel manageable again.

The catch is that all of this has to play out against a rough macro setup and some of the fiercest competition the QSR space has seen in years.

Cheap Enough?

Back in my last write-up, I genuinely thought the stock was a steal, assuming the business would find its footing by FY 2025. That never really played out. Profitability slid, leverage crept higher, and instead of getting cheaper, the multiples quietly stretched.

So the real question now is what comes next.

As I told you before, management projected an adjusted EBITDA between $225 and $240 million. In theory, if we consider deleveraging and an EV of ~$3.39 billion, that would give us multiples of ~15x EBITDA.

Not very interesting, is it?

But I'd really like to look at the FCF side now.

If you assume an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of roughly 65%, FY 2026 would point to OCF around $150 million. Even if Capex stays elevated, but cools off a bit into the $70 to $80 million range, that still leaves something like $75 million of FCF.

At that point, you're really only getting about a 2% FCF yield.

That puts Jack swimming in the same pond as a name like Yum! Brands, a business that actually earns the right to trade at a premium.

So if the stock doesn't exactly look like a steal right here and now, maybe it's time to think a little further out.

If you take the consensus revenue for FY 2028 at $1.16 billion and assume an EBITDA margin around 20%, you land at roughly $232 million in EBITDA.

Add in $75 million a year of deleveraging and $51.5 million in cash, and net debt would fall to about $1.22 billion. Even without share buybacks, and discounted back at a cost of equity close to 12%, that points to a price north of $50 a share when we model a 12x EBITDA multiple.

So the upside is still pretty juicy if they can redevelop, raise the margins, and get traffic moving again—which, fair enough, wasn't happening in December (latest geolocation-based traffic data has been released).

In the near term, I’d rather see the stock trading at a discount to peers like Restaurant Brands International, around 11x EBITDA, given its risk profile.

That points to a short-term price target of about $30.

A 36% upside is nothing to sneeze at, but you’d still be facing all those headwinds I outlined earlier.

You could argue for a speculative buy at a real bottom, especially under $20, but right now, Jack In The Box really needs some catalyst, and it's far from obvious.

That said, I'm moving my rating to Hold for now.

I'll be keeping an eye on how the balance sheet continues to deleverage and whether traffic in California picks up.

I plan to do an earnings preview when Jack reports Q1 FY 2026, and I’d like to see some of the traffic weakness ease before I start looking at this stock more favorably again. Even if traffic doesn't come back, I'd still expect a re-rating if we see cost cuts and margin improvements, but it would just take longer.

data.title: EMHY: Good High-Yield Bond ETF, But Expenses Are Too High To Buy
data.description:
I've written several articles on dollar-denominated emerging market debt funds these past few weeks, as these securities offer comparatively strong yields right now. Although I was able to find a good ETF focusing on quality emerging market bonds, I've been unable to find a good one focusing on high-yield emerging market bonds. Understandable, considering this is such a niche sub-asset class.

I thought the iShares J.P. Morgan EM High Yield Bond ETF (EMHY) might fit the bill, with the fund sporting an average 6.5% dividend yield, and above-average performance track-record. Although EMHY compares favorably to broader high-yield bond ETFs on several metrics, it's 0.50% expense ratio is a significant drag on its income and performance, and all but eliminates any advantage it has to its cheaper peers. As such, I will not be investing in the fund.

Emerging Market Bond Investment Thesis

A quick look at the investment thesis for dollar-denominated emerging market bonds before looking into EMHY itself.

The Federal Reserve hiked rates quite aggressively from 2022 to 2023, leading to a significant, sustained increase in yields across fixed-income asset classes Although rates and yields are down since, these remain somewhat elevated.

Some investments benefited more from Fed policy than others, some less, with U.S. high-yield bonds seeing their yields increase by a measly 0.2%. Dollar-denominated emerging market bonds, on the other hand, saw their dividend yields increase by 1.3%, much higher growth.

Such a disparity meant that emerging market bonds went from yielding 0.7% less than high-yield bonds, to 0.4% more and are, in fact, the second-highest yielding income investment in the market right now. Only senior loans yield more, but these are variable rate investments, so their yields should drop as the Fed continues to cut rates. Data as per JPMorgan:

Obvious implication of the above is to focus on dollar-denominated emerging market bond funds. These should offer investors above-average dividend yields and could see some gains if yields or spreads were to normalize. ETFs focusing on high-yield emerging market bonds could see particularly strong performance, which is where EMHY comes in. Let's have a look.

EMHY - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

EMHY is an index ETF investing in dollar-denominated, high-yield emerging market bonds. It includes both sovereign and corporate bonds, leaning toward the former.

It invests in over a dozen countries, overweighting Turkey and Brazil. I'm seeing small investments in China and Taiwan, of particular importance considering the rhetoric from China about taking back Taiwan.

EMHY's country diversification reduces risk, volatility, and the magnitude of losses due to defaults or issues with any individual issuer or country. Of particular importance considering the risks of emerging market countries, including political and regulatory risk.

Credit and Rate Risk

EMHY invests in high-yield bonds, focusing on securities rated BB, with sizable investments in those rated B.

Credit quality seems comparable to that of most high-yield corporate bond ETFs. For reference, credit quality for the iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY), the largest ETF in this niche.

Based on credit quality, EMHY could see sizable losses during downturns and recessions, close to those of USHY and most other high-yield corporate bonds.

As EMHY focuses on emerging market bonds, losses could be a bit higher than peers, due to sentiment if nothing else. As an example, EMHY suffered a drawdown of almost 30% during early 2020, compared to around 20% for USHY. By late 2020 sentiment had somewhat improved, and underperformance significantly narrowed. I would expect broadly similar results during any future downturn, with the caveat that the pandemic was a particularly brutal, sharp downturn.

Rate risk is moderate, with the fund sporting a duration of 5.0 years. In general, high-yield bonds tend to be issued at below-average maturities, as investors are generally reticent to extend long-term credit to riskier issuers. As an example, USHY has a duration of only 2.8, close to half that of EMHY. I believe that this is because EMHY invests quite heavily in sovereign bonds, which do tend to have longer maturities.

Expect to see moderate losses when rates increase, moderate gains when these go down. Price movements should be lower than those of its high-yield peers. As an example, rates skyrocketed from early 2022 to late 2023, during which EMHY's price declined by 15.6%, USHY's price was down 11.8%. Results were actually a bit better than expected, likely due to bearish sentiment (everything got sold off regardless of fundamentals).

Dividend Analysis

As mentioned previously, dollar-denominated emerging market bonds have some of the highest yields on the market right now, around 0.4% higher than high-yield corporate bonds.

EMHY's 6.5% dividend yield seems, technically, marginally higher than the high-yield corporate bond average. As the differences are small, and as different ETFs have different yields, figures are indistinguishable from the average and could easily be the result of volatility.

On the other hand, EMHY's 7.0% yield to maturity is consistently higher than those of its peers, with yield to maturities in the 6.5%-6.6% range. Results are broadly consistent with JPMorgan figures and spreads.

In my opinion, the results above are close to being enough for a buy rating, but there is an issue here: EMHY's 0.50% expense ratio. Although the fund's expenses are not excessive, and aren't even significantly above-average, they are higher than several high-yield corporate bonds. These include USHY, with a 0.08% expense ratio. For these two funds, the difference in expenses is more or less the same as the difference in yield to maturities, canceling each other out. Dollar-denominated emerging market bonds, as an asset class, trade with a compelling yield. EMHY itself, not so much.

Performance Track-Record

EMHY's performance track-record is reasonably good, although nothing special. It has outperformed bond ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes since inception, matching the performance of high-yield corporate bonds.

Stronger returns at the 3y mark:

In my opinion, the results above are broadly positive for EMHY, better than they appear at first glance.

EMHY performed a bit better than expected these past five years, because it matched the performance of high-yield corporate bonds even though it has a higher duration than these, and rates have increased in this time period. Conditions were somewhat unfavorable for the fund, returns about average, so results are encouraging.

At the same time, EMHY outperformed high-yield corporate bonds by around 4.0% per year these past three years, vastly outpacing the difference in dividend yields between them. In this case, rates were down so EMHY's higher duration was a positive.

In my opinion, in this particular case long-term results are less important than more recent ones, because conditions were much different before, less favorable to emerging market bonds. Since yields and spreads rose, EMHY has outperformed, and that could continue to be the case moving forward. I'm still wary of the expenses though.

Conclusion

EMHY has a reasonable investment thesis, centered on the attractive yield on its dollar-denominated, emerging market bond portfolio, and strong medium-term performance. On the other hand, the fund's 0.50% expense ratio is a material drag on its dividend yield and performance, and all but eliminates the income differential. I could see EMHY outperforming its high-yield peers if spreads normalize, but I'm not sure that this is enough for a buy rating. As such, I will not be investing in the fund.
